claims.text: In 1908 explorer G.E. Kincaid discovered a man-made cavern entrance roughly 2,000 feet up the Grand Canyon wall about 40 miles upriver from El Tovar Crystal Canyon, with hundreds of carved sandstone steps leading to it and walls inscribed with what appeared to be ancient Egyptian hieroglyphics.

evidence.quote:
in 1908 president Teddy Roosevelt wanted
to declare the Grand Canyon off limits
to all Timber and Mining operations it
would take another 11 years for Congress
to designate the Grand Canyon a national
park sensing a final opportunity for
ad

claims.rationale: Roosevelt's 1908 Grand Canyon designation as a National Monument is real, and Grand Canyon NP was created in 1919 — so the framing dates check. The Kincaid discovery itself, however, has no independent evidence. Kincaid is not in any explorers' registry; no expedition records exist; the Smithsonian denies all knowledge. The host's verdict implicitly accepts this is suspicious. directTruth low.

claims.text: Inside the cavern Kincaid found a vast underground complex with rooms entered by oval doors, ventilation shafts, three-foot-six walls, side passages branching at engineering-grade right angles, and capacity for 50,000 inhabitants — including granaries, glazed pottery still containing seeds, cooking areas, a huge dining hall, a metalworking room with copper-hardening technology unknown elsewhere, and a crypt full of male mummies.

evidence.quote:
50 000 people
could have lived here in some rooms he
found granaries with shelves of glazed
Pottery many of which still contain
seeds he found cooking areas and a huge
dining hall the rooms were full of
ancient artifacts Kincaid carefully
w

Lost
Art of hardening this medal which has
been sought by chemicals for centuries
without result on a bench running around
a work room with some charcoal and other
material properly used in the process
there's also slag and stuff similar to

claims.rationale: These details all come from the 1909 Phoenix Gazette article, which the host's own verdict reveals is the only source. No surviving artifacts, no Smithsonian archive entry, no second visit ever documented. The 'lost art of hardening copper' claim is suspicious — copper hardening (work-hardening, alloying with arsenic or tin into bronze) was known to many ancient cultures including Andean and Old Kingdom Egyptians. Single-newspaper-source extraordinary archaeology with no replicable physical site is the textbook fingerprint of a hoax.

claims.text: After Kincaid's discovery, Smithsonian Professor S.A. Jordan led a team of about 40 scientists and laborers into the complex, found a central cross-hall containing a Buddha-like idol with lotus flowers and an Oriental cast suggestive of ancient Tibetan religious practice, named the complex 'The Citadel', and concluded that the original civilization originated in Egypt or Asia and predated Native Americans by thousands of years.

evidence.quote:
Professor S.A
Jordan arrived with a team of about 40
scientists researchers and laborers to
excavate and explore the ancient
underground city now with more lights
and Manpower the scientists realized
that the cave system layout wasn't
rando

sitting cross-legged
with a lotus flower or lily in each hand
the cast of this face is Oriental and
the carving this Cavern the idol almost
resembles Buddha though the scientists
are not certain as to what religious
worship it represents ta

claims.rationale: The Smithsonian PR officer Marion (in the host's own phone-call segment) flatly states no person named S.A. Jordan ever worked for the Smithsonian. There is no Smithsonian publication, expedition log, or institutional record of this 40-person mission. The chimerical 'Egyptian + Buddhist + Tibetan' culture mash-up is more characteristic of late-Victorian armchair speculation than of anything found by trained archaeologists.

claims.text: After Kincaid and Jordan sent boxes of artifacts and notes back to the Smithsonian along with a hypothesis that an advanced trans-oceanic civilization predated Native Americans, their request for further funding was denied, and both Kincaid and Jordan were never heard from again — a sudden silencing the host treats as suspicious.

evidence.quote:
never heard from again
[Music]
in northern Arizona is one of the most
spectacular natural wonders on Earth the
Grand Canyon carved over millions of
years by the Colorado River the Grand
Canyon is more than just a pretty
landscape and a spot

claims.rationale: The 'never heard from again' framing is rhetorical. The simpler explanation is they never existed in the first place — no birth/death records, no other expeditions, no contemporary obituaries for either G.E. Kincaid or S.A. Jordan have ever been found. People who don't exist have a tendency to never be heard from.

claims.text: All the information about the Citadel comes from two front-page articles in the Phoenix Gazette published in 1909, and mainstream researchers have decided the articles were a hoax — '19th-century clickbait designed to sell newspapers' — though many people believe the story is real and that the Smithsonian has been covering it up ever since.

evidence.quote:
Two Front Page articles from
the Phoenix Gazette published in 1909
and the years since mainstream
researchers have decided that the
Articles were a hoax a fantastic story
designed to sell newspapers in 19th
century clickbait yep the media h

claims.rationale: The April 5, 1909 Phoenix Gazette story is real — that's the entire textual provenance. No second source, no independent contemporaneous corroboration, no follow-up reporting. Mainstream archaeology and the Smithsonian itself uniformly classify it as a hoax. Yellow journalism in early-1900s American newspapers regularly published wholly invented 'discoveries' for circulation, so the hoax classification fits the era's journalism patterns.

claims.text: When the host called the Smithsonian, PR representative Marion stated firmly that 'no Egyptian-style artifacts of any kind have ever been found in North or South America', no one named G.E. Kincaid is recorded as having worked for the Smithsonian, and 'the whole thing is a hoax' — confirming the institution's official position.

evidence.quote:
Smithsonian public relations
I'm here I'm here sorry I was on hold a
long time
um they transferred me to Marion yes
this is Marion okay great my name is AJ
and I'm with the y-files we're a YouTube
channel that covers Mysteries
Van you, the Y

claims.rationale: The phone-call segment is real and the Smithsonian's stance is consistent with public statements they have made for decades on this topic. The host treats this as confirmation-of-cover-up but it is more naturally read as institutional denial of a fabricated story. Either way, the factual claim that the Smithsonian denies the discovery is correct.

claims.text: Hopi mythology says that thousands of years ago a reptilian race emerged from the Grand Canyon and changed into humans (lizard people / shape-shifters), and other Pueblo cultures (Zuni, Acoma) have similar legends about a non-human race that lived in the area before the tribes arrived.

evidence.quote:
a reptilian race
emerged
yep hey how many times do lizard people
have to come up in these stories before
you believe in him I I guess I'm gonna
need a few more lizard people are gonna
keep popping up you'll see thousands of
years ago a rept

claims.rationale: Hopi emergence narrative does describe ancestors emerging from a sipapu (place of emergence) traditionally associated with the Grand Canyon's Sipapuni travertine dome. The 'snake clan' (Tsu'utsu) is part of Hopi clan structure. However, framing this as 'reptilian shape-shifters → modern lizard-people conspiracy' is the host overlaying David Icke-style conspiracy frames onto an indigenous origin story. The Hopi narrative is real; the lizard-people gloss is not how Hopi tradition describes it.

claims.text: The Hopi 'Ant People' legend describes humanoid creatures with pale skin and large insect-like eyes who sheltered the Hopi underground during catastrophes around the Younger Dryas era, and a parallel Hopi/Pueblo legend about the Hav-musuvs describes golden-skinned beings who flew silver canoes ('UFOs') and possessed lightning-firing silver tubes — both legends suggesting ancient extraterrestrial contact.

evidence.quote:
ant people
these are humanoid creatures that are
smaller than humans with pale skin and
large eyes like an insect aliens
according to Legend a series of
cataclysms hit the earth many thousands
of years ago young adrias the Hopi were
on the

have a legend about the have
mussoos they're usually described as
being humanoid in shape with golden skin
and large eyes aliens they lived in a
vast underground city and traded with
Far Away people they possessed weapons

that shot lightning and can kill a man
instantly bases and the most interesting
that the most interesting

claims.rationale: The 'Ant People' (Anu Sinom in Hopi) do appear in Hopi narrative tradition as benevolent underground helpers. The 'Hav-musuvs' / 'Hav-musuvs of Death Valley' is a much more fringe attribution — its origin is the 1940s/50s writings of Bourke Lee and later UFO authors, not core Pueblo oral tradition. Reading both as 'aliens' is straightforward late-20th-century ancient-aliens reframing not present in source traditions.

claims.text: The forbidden Grand Canyon zone above the alleged Citadel contains rock formations bearing names from ancient Egyptian (Isis Temple, Tower of Set, Horus, Cheops Pyramid, Ra) and Hindu (Manu Temple, Buddha Temple, Krishna Temple, Sheba Temple) cultures — cultures that, the host notes, share myths of a god helping civilization after the great flood — and the official 'coincidence' explanation strains credulity.

evidence.quote:
Isis Temple Tower of set Horus the kiops
pyramid and raw there's also the Manu
Temple the Buddha temple the Krishna
Temple and the Sheba Temple and many
more names that refer to ancient
Egyptian and Hindu cultures cultures
that specifically have myths about a God
helping civilization after the great
flood the official word is the fact that
these

claims.rationale: The features really exist with these names — they were named in the 1880s by U.S. Geological Survey geographer Clarence Dutton, who explicitly chose 'temples' from world religions as a poetic naming convention for monumental erosion features. The host treats the names as evidence of buried Egyptian/Hindu sites; the documented historical fact is they were chosen by Dutton's romantic comparative-religion sensibility, not because the features resemble or contain those cultures' artifacts. The 'coincidence' explanation is actually well-attested.

claims.text: Modern attempts to find the cave (Jerry and Kathy Wills' research, multiple expeditions) have been blocked by the federal government: the area is designated off-limits, about 970 of the Grand Canyon's ~1,000 caves are unmapped or sealed, expedition teams have been buzzed by unmarked planes and Apache combat helicopters in a no-fly zone, and unexplained cement platforms with mountaineering anchors have been found at the rim.

evidence.quote:
an Apache combat helicopter
when this team explored the area above
looking for the ventilation shafts
Kincaid wrote about and possibly a way
down they found cement blocks in the
middle of nowhere some of these blocks
are platforms near the

claims.rationale: Most of the Grand Canyon's caves are indeed unmapped and many are restricted (legitimately for ecological / safety / Native American sacred-site reasons). 'Apache combat helicopter' enforcement claims come from the Wills' own book and have no independent corroboration. Federal cave-restriction policies are routine and not specific to this site. The cement-platform-with-anchor sightings are not independently documented.

claims.text: The Smithsonian has been actively suppressing pre-Columbian archaeological evidence since 1881 when geologist John Wesley Powell appointed Cyrus Thomas to the Bureau of Ethnology — Thomas pivoted the institution from diffusionism (which posits widespread cultural contact) to isolationism (which posits civilizations developed separately) — and has hidden multiple finds of giant skeletons including the 1930s Spiro Mounds (7-8 ft tall), 1892 Alabama stone coffins (7.5 ft long), and WWII-era Alaska airship-site mounds with skeletons two to three times normal size.

evidence.quote:
cover-up began in 1881 with famous
geologist John Wesley Powell Powell
appointed Cyrus Thomas the director of
the Eastern Mound division of the
Smithsonian institutions Bureau of
ethnology when Thomas came to the
Smithsonian he believed there was a race
of mound builders that were distinct
from and arrived before the American
Indians this school of thought is called
diffusionism which says throughout
history there's been a widespread
dispersion of culture and civilization
even across great distances the
Smithsonian took the opposite approach

Spiro Mounds
were excavated in the 1930s seven and
eight foot tall skeletons were found at
a time when the average height of a
human male was about five foot six those
skeletons have been lost in 1892 Stone
coffins were discovered in Alabama by
Frank Burns during a Geological Survey
the coffins were about seven and a half
feet long hollowed out by fire and metal
chisels the findings were sent to the
Smithsonian in 1984 this report w

World War II a crew was building
an airship in Alaska they excavated a
mound that had gigantic human remains
skeletons twice or three times the size
of a normal human this report has been
confirmed by multiple sources the
Smithsonian collec

claims.rationale: John Wesley Powell did head the Bureau of Ethnology starting 1879 (close to 1881) and Cyrus Thomas was a real ethnologist who published on Mound Builders. But the host has the historical pivot inverted: Cyrus Thomas's 1894 'Report on Mound Explorations' actually DEMOLISHED the racist 'separate Mound Builder race' theory by demonstrating American Indians built the mounds — the opposite of what the host claims. 'Giant skeletons' newspaper reports from the 1800s-1900s are well-studied; almost all trace to misidentified mastodon bones, deliberate hoaxes (Cardiff Giant), or measurement errors. The 'Smithsonian basement of giant skeletons' is a long-debunked Vine Deloria/Childress conspiracy with no archaeological corroboration.

claims.text: French author David Hatcher Childress has published 200 books on ancient mysteries and argues that the U.S. government — particularly the Smithsonian — actively suppresses archaeological discoveries, with the warehouse scene at the end of Raiders of the Lost Ark being closer to reality than fiction.

evidence.quote:
David Hatcher Childress is a French
author who's published 200 books he
often covers ancient Mysteries he
believes the United States government is
actively suppressing archaeological
discoveries he mentions that while the
film Raiders of the Lost Ark is fiction
the final scene showing the warehouses
close to reality that the Smithsonian
and independent Federal agency is hiding
some of the most important discoveries
made in the Americas he says the
cover-up began in 1881 with famous
geologist John Wesley Powell Powell
appointed Cyrus Thomas the director

claims.rationale: Childress is a real author (American, not French — host's nationality detail is wrong) who has published roughly 200 books, mostly through his own Adventures Unlimited Press. He is the most prolific publisher of ancient-aliens / lost-civilization material in English. His specific claims about Smithsonian suppression have been examined and rejected by mainstream archaeology. The Raiders 'warehouse' scene is, of course, fiction. directTruth low because the conspiracy framework is unsupported.

claims.text: Hopi tradition holds that when the first people emerged from the Grand Canyon they sent a messenger to the temple of Sun for a blessing of peace; the messenger has not yet returned, but Hopi village elders still gather on rooftops at sundown awaiting his return — when he does, the ancient land will be returned to the first people.

evidence.quote:
the Hopi say that when the first people emerged
from the Grand Canyon they sent a
message to the temple of son asking for
a blessing of peace that messenger
hasn't returned but even today in Hopi
Villages at sundown tribal Elders can be
seen on rooftops gazing toward the
Setting Sun waiting for the messenger to

claims.rationale: Hopi emergence and Pahana / 'True White Brother' return narratives are well-attested in Hopi oral tradition (Frank Waters' 'Book of the Hopi', 1963, and earlier ethnographic records). The 'messenger to the temple of Sun' framing is one variant; the rooftop-watching detail at sundown is more poetic license than precise ethnography. The Pahana return prophecy is a real Hopi belief; specific liturgical practices vary by village.
